Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore
Lawyers: Yeo Teng Yung Christopher (Legal Solutions LLC) for the plaintiff
Lavan Vickneson (Legal Solutions LLC) for the plaintiff

Party_Details.Name: AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd (in Liquidation)

Facts.Text: The liquidator has brought this action against the defendants for having authorised or allowed withdrawals from the plaintiff company’s bank account.
The plaintiff is AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd, a company incorporated in Singapore with the objects of trading in metals and providing financial services.
The plaintiff was wound up on 9 March 2018 based on an unsatisfied statutory demand dated 22 November 2017 in the amount of US$2,145,000.
The action is brought by Mr Abuthahir s/o Abdul Gafoor, the liquidator of AMG, from AAG Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd.
The petitioning creditor in the winding up was Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd, a company incorporated in Thailand.
From October 2007 onwards, AMG had three directors together, namely Mr Manu, Mr Ong and Mr Koh.
For many years AMG did very little business but incurred expenses including rental of premises.
Mr Manu talked about potential deals for AMG but these did not materialise for many years.
On 25 August 2017, AMG’s account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited received a deposit of US$2,764,104.53 arising from a deal with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd.
Prior to a small deposit of US$7.22 on 23 August 2017, the balance in AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account had been zero.

Under a discounting and monetizing memorandum of understanding dated 21 June 2017 between AMG and Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd, AMG was to receive an irrevocable confirmed documentary letter of credit in the sum of US$2,860,000 issued by Kasikornbank Public Company Limited of Bangkok, Thailand.
Under the memorandum of understanding, AMG was to pay 75% of the value of the letter of credit, namely US$2,145,000, back to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd within three business days.
Under the memorandum of understanding, AMG was to pay 5% of the value of the letter of credit, namely US$143,000, to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited as a service fee.
Under the memorandum of understanding, the remaining 20% of the value of the letter of credit, namely US$572,000, was described as non-recourse payment for AMG.

Under the arrangement with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd, AMG was on paper the seller of 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets and was also to act as discounter or monetizer of the documentary letter of credit.
The court inferred that Mr Manu on behalf of AMG presented false delivery documents to Kasikornbank in order to receive payment of US$2,860,000 under the documentary letter of credit.
AMG did not supply Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd with the 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets referred to in the documentary letter of credit.
AMG did not pay the agreed sum of US$2,145,000 to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd under the memorandum of understanding.
Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd pursued payment of US$2,145,000 by issuing a statutory demand on 22 November 2017.

As a result of share allotments in September and October 2006, AMG originally had 100,000 shares held by Mr Ong, 1,080,000 shares registered in the name of Mr Koh, and 100,000 shares held by a third person.

Of the 1,080,000 shares registered in Mr Koh’s name, 1,079,996 shares were held by him as nominee on trust for Weeds Natural Remedies Pte Ltd in consideration of Weeds providing formulae for natural health supplements and an eight-year licence to exclusive use of certain registered trade marks.
A directors’ resolution dated 21 September 2017 showed that at that time 640,000 shares in AMG were registered in Mr Ong’s name.
A directors’ resolution dated 25 September 2017 recorded a transfer of 640,000 shares in AMG from Mr Koh to Mr Manu.
By 23 January 2018 at least, Mr Manu had become the 100% owner of AMG.
AMG appears to have had no assets other than the balance in its Standard Chartered Bank account and no business that might be a source of revenue.
Once the balance in AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account fell below US$2,145,000, the company could no longer pay its debt to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd and was insolvent.
AMG reached the state of insolvency on or about 29 August 2017.
Further large payments and withdrawals from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account continued after it became insolvent.
At trial AMG was represented by counsel, while both Mr Ong and Mr Koh appeared as litigants-in-person.
At the start of trial Mr Ong had not filed any affidavit of evidence-in-chief but was nonetheless allowed to give oral evidence subject to cross-examination.
AMG called as witnesses only the liquidator and his assistant, who relied on documents in AMG’s possession or obtained from Standard Chartered Bank.
AMG’s case is that Mr Ong breached his director’s duties in respect of payments made out of the Standard Chartered Bank account between 25 August 2017 and 21 September 2017.
AMG’s case is that Mr Koh breached his director’s duties in respect of payments made out of the Standard Chartered Bank account between 28 September 2017 and 2 July 2018.

AMG alleges that none of the impugned payments provided commercial or other benefit, whether actual or apparent, to the company and that the defendants did not check or verify the transactions at all.
AMG pleaded knowing receipt against Mr Ong in respect of US$74,699.11 and against Mr Koh in respect of US$74,706.20.
In closing submissions AMG also advanced dishonest assistance as a basis of liability, although this was not pleaded.
AMG relied on the general law concerning directors’ duties and on section 157 of the Companies Act to frame the duties owed by the defendants.

The court identified the principal duties of a director in relation to company transactions as acting bona fide in the interests of the company, acting for a proper corporate purpose, avoiding conflicts between interest and duty, and exercising reasonable skill and care.

The court found that a director authorising a payment must believe that the payment is for a proper purpose of the company and must take reasonable steps to understand the transaction and supporting documentation.

The court noted that under section 145(5) of the Companies Act a director of a company cannot resign or vacate office unless at least one director ordinarily resident in Singapore remains in the company.

The court set out the elements of knowing receipt as disposal of company assets in breach of fiduciary duty, beneficial receipt of those assets by the defendant, and knowledge making it unconscionable for the defendant to retain the assets.
The court found that Mr Ong fully understood his actions while a director of AMG and could not be excused from liability by reason of alleged mental infirmity.
The court found that AMG’s insolvency meant that its directors had to consider the interests of creditors when authorising payments from the company’s funds.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether AMG’s former directors, Mr Ong and Mr Koh, breached their fiduciary and statutory duties and incurred liability, including for knowing receipt, in relation to withdrawals and payments made from AMG’s bank account and the use of a bank facility after receipt of funds under a documentary letter of credit transaction with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act bona fide in the interests of the company, for proper corporate purposes, avoid conflicts between personal interest and duty, and exercise reasonable skill and care under the general law and section 157 of the Companies Act; a director authorising payments must honestly and reasonably believe they are for proper corporate purposes and take reasonable steps to understand and verify the transactions; once a company is approaching insolvency, directors must consider the interests of creditors; knowing receipt requires a disposal of company assets in breach of fiduciary duty, beneficial receipt by the defendant, and knowledge making it unconscionable to retain the assets; under section 145(5) of the Companies Act a director cannot resign if no other locally resident director remains; under section 391 of the Companies Act the court may relieve a director from liability if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the structure of AMG’s monetizing arrangement with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd and the subsequent flows of funds through AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account, finding that AMG did not perform its obligations to supply aluminium or to remit the agreed sums to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd and that the company’s only real asset was the bank balance generated by the letter of credit transaction; the court accepted that the liquidator, lacking first-hand knowledge, relied on bank and company records to reconstruct the transactions and to identify payments that lacked any apparent commercial purpose or supporting documentation; the court applied the identified duties of directors to the specific withdrawals and payments authorised or allowed by Mr Ong and Mr Koh, assessing whether each director honestly believed the payments were for proper corporate purposes and whether such belief was reasonable in light of the circumstances, including the size of the payments, the absence of documentation, the company’s insolvency, and the directors’ knowledge of Mr Manu’s conduct; the court treated the pleaded knowing receipt claims as overlapping with primary fiduciary liability, except for the payment to Mr Koh before he rejoined the board, and evaluated whether he received company funds in circumstances where he knew they were being misapplied; the court considered but ultimately rejected relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, concluding that Mr Ong’s acquiescence to improper payments after he realised their nature was not reasonable and that Mr Koh’s decision to give Mr Manu a free rein in return for repayment of his loans was neither honest nor reasonable given the obvious risk of asset stripping and the company’s insolvency.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court held that AMG, acting by its liquidator, had established breaches of duty and knowing receipt in respect of specified payments and withdrawals from its bank account and misuse of a bank facility, and that neither section 145(5) nor section 391 of the Companies Act excused the defendants from liability; AMG was awarded monetary relief with interest against each defendant in the amounts determined by the court.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore
Lawyers: Yeo Teng Yung Christopher (Legal Solutions LLC) for the plaintiff
Lavan Vickneson (Legal Solutions LLC) for the plaintiff

Party_Details.Name: Ong Kee Ming Richard

Facts.Text: The first defendant, Mr Ong Kee Ming Richard, is a Singapore citizen.
Mr Ong was a director of AMG from 13 September 2006 to 25 September 2017.
From October 2007 onwards, Mr Ong served as a director of AMG together with Mr Manu and Mr Koh.
A directors’ resolution dated 21 September 2017 showed that 640,000 shares in AMG were registered in Mr Ong’s name at that time.
During the period 23 August 2017 to 21 September 2017, Mr Ong and Mr Manu were joint signatories to AMG’s account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited.
For about a decade before 2017, Mr Ong and Mr Koh had hoped that their fellow director, Mr Manu, would secure a major deal for AMG.
After Mr Koh left AMG in early 2014, Mr Ong continued as director with Mr Manu.
In August 2017, Mr Manu secured a deal with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd that resulted in millions of dollars flowing into AMG’s bank account.
After the funds from the Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd deal were received, Mr Ong signed off on a series of withdrawals from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account.
Mr Ong later suffered a nervous collapse and resigned from his directorship in AMG.
Mr Ong asserted that during the time of his alleged breaches of duty he was in poor physical and mental health.
Mr Ong described Mr Manu as a friend and business partner of many years whom he trusted to conduct AMG’s business and handle its bank account.
Mr Ong stated that he relied on Mr Manu’s representations that payments from AMG’s bank account were legitimate business payments and that Mr Manu would handle the accounting records.
Mr Ong claimed that Mr Manu intimidated him by raising his voice and insisting that he countersign payment documents, taking advantage of his weak health.

On or about 11 August 2017, Mr Ong obtained from Mr Manu a handwritten confirmation that Mr Manu took full responsibility for payments made from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account and for the procurement and supply of goods mentioned in the documentary letter of credit.
The court found that the handwritten confirmation obtained by Mr Ong showed that he understood his potential liability and the importance of taking steps to protect himself.

A memo from Clementi Polyclinic dated 26 February 2020 recorded that Mr Ong had visited the clinic in September 2017 and was referred to the National University Hospital to investigate a possible psychosis episode.
The same memo recorded that Mr Ong was later seen by the psychiatric team at the National University Hospital with an impression of psychotic depression.
Mr Ong testified that he was warded at the Institute of Mental Health for three weeks from 15 October 2017.
The court accepted that Mr Ong was stressed to the point of mental collapse by the demands placed on him by Mr Manu and that he was psychologically pressured to comply with signing withdrawals.

The court found that it was Mr Ong’s realisation that he had signed off on withdrawals and payments that were not for proper purposes of AMG, together with continued pressure from Mr Manu, that caused his mental collapse.
The court concluded that Mr Ong fully understood his actions while he was a director of AMG and could not be excused from liability by reason of mental infirmity.
Mr Ong raised allegations of duress and deceit against Mr Manu, asserting that he was manipulated and oppressed into signing payment documents.
Mr Ong contended that some of the payments he signed were valid payments for the benefit of AMG.
In response to a letter of demand dated 21 February 2020, Mr Ong wrote on 2 March 2020 explaining that he relied on Mr Manu’s representations and felt intimidated into countersigning payments.
Mr Ong countersigned nineteen withdrawals from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account during the relevant period.
Together with Mr Manu, Mr Ong signed a security agreement with Standard Chartered Bank in relation to a facility secured against a fixed deposit of US$1,000,000.
On 25 August 2017, Mr Ong countersigned a cash withdrawal of US$11,216.75 from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account for Mr Manu’s personal expenses.
On 25 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$22,556.39 from AMG’s account to ORO Trading Pte Ltd, which he understood to be repayment of a deposit for a failed gold trade.
On 26 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$9,398.50 to Mr Lim Chin Keng for air tickets for Mr Manu to travel to Africa and India, purportedly on AMG’s business.
On 26 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$22,142.86 to Ms Chen Mei Qi, which he understood to be repayment of a personal loan to Mr Manu.
On 26 August 2017, Mr Ong countersigned a further cash withdrawal of US$20,000 from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account without a clear explanation of its purpose.
By the time of the US$20,000 cash withdrawal on 26 August 2017, Mr Ong had begun to have concerns but still relied on Mr Manu’s assurances that the transactions would be accounted for.
On 28 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$149,398.22 from AMG’s account to Mr Abdul Razeed.

In relation to the US$149,398.22 payment to Mr Abdul Razeed, Mr Ong testified that he objected to the transaction, told Mr Manu he wanted to step down as a director, and expressed that the payments involved were too large.
The court found that the payment to Mr Abdul Razeed on 28 August 2017 marked a turning point at which Mr Ong understood that Mr Manu was likely stripping AMG of funds.
On 28 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$74,699.11 from AMG’s account to himself, which he regarded as remuneration for his services as a director, including past years’ service.
On 28 August 2017, Mr Ong countersigned a large cash withdrawal of US$112,666.38 from AMG’s Standard Chartered Bank account.
On 28 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$5,000 from AMG’s account to Mr Adanuty, which he understood to be repayment of a personal loan of Mr Manu.
On 29 August 2017 and 5 September 2017, Mr Ong signed three payments totalling US$181,119.92 from AMG’s account to Sabre Global Pte Ltd, two of which were described as consultancy fees.
On 29 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$75,000 from AMG’s account to Mr Manu, which was described as remuneration for past directors’ fees.
On 29 August 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$100,000 from AMG’s account to Three Quality Services Limited, purportedly for consultancy fees.
On 5 September 2017 and 21 September 2017, Mr Ong signed payments totalling US$450,000 from AMG’s account to Mr Kevin Anthony Griffin Jr, with transfer forms referring to the supply of aluminium.

On 5 September 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$143,000 from AMG’s account to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited, corresponding to the 5% service fee stipulated in the memorandum of understanding with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd.
On 6 September 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$150,000 from AMG’s account to Quality Printers Pte Ltd, later said by that company to be for transport services.
On 21 September 2017, Mr Ong signed a payment of US$37,458.77 from AMG’s account to Mr Koh, which was the first half of an intended US$75,000 remuneration to Mr Koh.
Mr Ong testified that he was reluctant to countersign some of the payments, including the US$75,000 payment to Mr Manu, but did so under pressure.
The court found that from 28 August 2017 onwards Mr Ong understood or ought to have understood that payments requested by Mr Manu were not for proper purposes of AMG.

Mr Ong believed that as the only Singapore-resident director he could not resign unilaterally because of the requirement in section 145(5) of the Companies Act for at least one locally resident director.
Mr Ong eventually refused to continue signing payment documents, which led to Mr Manu arranging for Mr Koh to return as the locally resident director.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Mr Ong, as a director and joint bank signatory of AMG, breached his fiduciary and statutory duties and incurred liability, including for knowing receipt, in relation to specific withdrawals and payments from AMG’s bank account and the establishment of a bank facility, notwithstanding his claims of poor mental health, intimidation by a co-director, and reliance on that co-director’s assurances.
Party_Details.Rule: A director must act bona fide in the interests of the company, for proper corporate purposes, avoid conflicts between personal interest and duty, and exercise reasonable skill and care under the general law and section 157 of the Companies Act; when authorising payments, a director must honestly believe they are for proper corporate purposes and must take reasonable steps to understand and verify the transactions, with the extent of inquiry depending on the circumstances and size of the transaction; a director may rely on information from fellow directors only if such reliance is both honest and reasonable and he maintains an enquiring mind rather than turning a blind eye to suspicious circumstances; once a company is approaching insolvency, directors must consider the interests of creditors when making decisions affecting company assets; under section 145(5) of the Companies Act, a director cannot resign if doing so would leave the company without a locally resident director, but while in office he remains fully subject to his duties; knowing receipt requires that company assets have been disposed of in breach of fiduciary duty, that the defendant beneficially received those assets, and that the defendant knew of the breach such that it is unconscionable to retain the benefit; under section 391 of the Companies Act, a director may be relieved from liability only if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused.
Party_Details.Application: The court accepted that Mr Ong initially had no reason to doubt Mr Manu’s honesty and that early payments, such as the repayment to ORO Trading Pte Ltd and travel expenses, could reasonably be viewed as legitimate in the context of a newly secured major deal, so those transactions were not treated as breaches; however, by 28 August 2017, particularly in relation to the large payment to Mr Abdul Razeed and substantial unexplained cash withdrawals, Mr Ong’s own testimony showed that he objected, wanted to resign, and recognised that the payments were too large and suspicious, indicating that he no longer honestly believed they were for proper corporate purposes; despite this realisation, he continued to countersign significant payments, including a large cash withdrawal of US$112,666.38, a US$5,000 payment to Mr Adanuty for a personal loan of Mr Manu, consultancy-labelled payments to Sabre Global Pte Ltd and Three Quality Services Limited without any supporting documentation, substantial transfers to Mr Kevin Anthony Griffin Jr purportedly for aluminium supply without evidence of underlying transactions, and a US$150,000 payment to Quality Printers Pte Ltd that was later justified as transport services inconsistent with AMG’s lack of staff, all of which the court found he could not reasonably have believed were for AMG’s benefit; the court held that the US$74,699.11 payment to Mr Ong himself, described as directors’ remuneration, lacked any proper basis or shareholder approval and was made when AMG was effectively insolvent, so it constituted unauthorised self-dealing and a breach of duty, and that Mr Ong ought reasonably to have appreciated that the payment was part of Mr Manu’s scheme to secure his cooperation; the court found that the payment of US$143,000 to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited matched the contractual service fee under the memorandum of understanding and was therefore a legitimate contractual payment, and that the establishment of the Standard Chartered Bank facility secured by a fixed deposit was a genuine banking transaction that Mr Ong could reasonably regard as beneficial to AMG’s trading, especially as the later misuse of the facility occurred after his resignation; the court rejected Mr Ong’s reliance on mental health issues and alleged intimidation as a defence, concluding that his mental collapse was a consequence of his participation in improper payments rather than a cause that negated his understanding, and that a reasonable director in his position, particularly after 28 August 2017, would have refused to countersign further withdrawals; the court also held that section 145(5) of the Companies Act did not excuse Mr Ong’s conduct, because although he could not unilaterally resign while he was the only locally resident director, he remained obliged to refuse improper payments and ultimately did secure his replacement by insisting on not signing further documents; the court considered but declined to grant relief under section 391, finding that Mr Ong’s continued authorisation of payments after he realised their improper nature was not reasonable, even if his conduct was understandable given human frailty.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court concluded that Mr Ong breached his fiduciary and statutory duties in relation to a series of payments and withdrawals from AMG’s bank account after 28 August 2017, including unauthorised self-remuneration, and that he was liable to AMG for losses totalling US$1,297,883.63 together with interest at 5.33% per annum from the date of the writ to the date of judgment; the court rejected any defence based on mental incapacity, intimidation, section 145(5), or section 391 of the Companies Act and did not find him liable in knowing receipt beyond his primary fiduciary breaches.
